name: CPython Bytecode
summary: Stack-based bytecode for CPython, with each opcode encoded as 1 byte (opcode) + 1 byte (oparg or extension). Modern CPython (3.11+) uses an adaptive interpreter: hot opcodes are rewritten in place to type-specialised variants based on runtime feedback (PEP 659).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: CPython's bytecode is stack-based, with each opcode encoded as 1 byte (opcode) + 1 byte (oparg or extension). Modern CPython (3.11+) uses an *adaptive* interpreter: hot opcodes are rewritten in place to type-specialised variants (`LOAD_ATTR_INSTANCE_VALUE` instead of generic `LOAD_ATTR`) based on runtime feedback. PEP 659 specifies this.

sections.title: Specialisation as bytecode substrate
sections.content:
The specialising adaptive interpreter is the most consequential CPython performance change in years: 10–60% runtime improvement without a JIT, by exploiting monomorphic call-site type stability inside the interpreter dispatch loop.

The bytecode design enables this: the in-place opcode rewriting works because CPython's bytecode is a contiguous byte array with stable instruction widths, and the runtime can swap one opcode for a specialised variant of the same width without disturbing surrounding code. This makes the bytecode itself the speculation substrate, with adaptive feedback recorded as instruction-level rewrites.
